content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
May 16,2016 
Alan Seem 
Legal Counsel 
Shearman & Sterling, LLP 
1460 El Camino Real, 2nd Floor 
Menlo Park, CA 94025-4110 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-16-079 
Dear Mr. Seem: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act")1 on behalf of the China Silicon Valley Business Development ("CSV"), a 
California non-profit corporation, and certain unnamed mayors. 
Note that the Act imposes duties on public officials and on donors to the officials.2 Because 
your questions are general in nature and you have not named officials on whose behalf you request 
advice, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not 
provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Finally, because the Fair Political Practices Commission (the 
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), this letter is based on the facts presented. 
FACTS 
You are writing to request advice related to the China Silicon Valley 2016 Mayors' China 
Trip (the "2016 China Trip") organized by CSV. You stated that CSV is organizing a trip for 
Silicon Valley mayors to travel to China as part of a 2016 China Trip. You stated that the purpose, 
sponsorship, and certain arrangements of the 2016 China Trip are similar to the Silicon Valley 
Mayors' China Trip CSV organized in June 2014 (the "2014 China Trip") and September 2015 (the 
"2015 China Trip"). 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Section 89521 provides: "Any person who makes or receives an honorarium, gift, or expenditure in violation 
of this chapter is liable in a civil action brought by the Commission for an amount of up to three times the amount of the 
unlawful honorarium, gift, or expenditure. 


File No. I-16-079 
Page No. 2 
The 2016 China Trip is aimed at improving business cooperation between China and the 
Silicon Valley and helping create jobs and increase economic activity in both China and the Silicon 
Valley. The principal purpose of the 2016 China Trip is to facilitate investment and international 
trade, and promote communications between China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China, 
the mayors will have opportunities to participate in meetings with local government officials, 
potential investors and CEOs from local high tech companies. The proposed itinerary provides: 
"The CSV 2016 Mayors China Trip wil1 depart from San Francisco on May 215\ 
visit Shanghai, Chongqing, Zhengzhou and Beijing. The itinerary is as follows: 
"May 22 
Evening: Arrive in Shanghai 
"May 23 
Morning: Event with officials and business leaders in Shanghai 
Afternoon: Visit science and technology park 
"May 24 
Morning: Flight to Chongqing 
Afternoon: Visit Chongqing City 
Evening: Formal Welcome Dinner 
"May 25 
Morning: Event with officials and business leaders in Chongqing 
Afternoon: Visit science and technology park 
"May 26 
Morning: Flight to Zhengzhou 
Afternoon: Visit Zhengzhou 
Evening: Formal Welcome Dinner 
"May 27 
Morning: Event with officials and business leaders in Zhengzhou 
Afternoon: Flight to Beijing 
"May 28 
Morning: Event with officials and business leaders in Beijing 
Afternoon: Visit Beijing 
Eve_ning: Cocktail Party starting at 6 pm 
"May 29 
Whole Day: Beijing International Fair For Trade In Services 
"May 30 
Morning: Visit science and technology park 
Afternoon: Flight back to San Francisco 


"May 31 (Optional) 
Morning: Visit Beijing City 
Afternoon: Flight back to San Francisco" 
With respect to financing, you stated: 
File No. 1-16-079 
Page No. 3 
• All international air travel expenses to and from China, approximately $2,000 per 
person, and the remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China during 
the 2016 China Trip, approximately $3,000 per person, will be provided by local 
Chinese government authorities, including the Zhangjiang Management Committee, 
which is the regulatory authority of Shanghai Pudong New District in Zhangjiang Hi­
Tech Park, People's Government of Yubei District in Chongqing, Zhengzhou Municipal 
People's Government, and Beijing Digital Content Industries Association, a non-profit 
organization supervised by Zhongguancun Tech Park Management Council and Haidian 
District Government in Beijing. 
• CSV will not provide any funds or services with monetary value to the mayors in 
connection with the 2016 China Trip. 
ANALYSIS 
I. Are tlze payments subject to the Gift Limits of the Act? 
A gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on 
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the 
regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status." In an effort to 
reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by local public 
officials in three ways: 
• First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is $460 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503; Regulation 
18940.2.) This gift limit applies to all Mayors, among other officials. (Sections 89503(a) & 
87200.) 
• Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials, including Mayors, requiring that 
any gift (or any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during 
the calendar year are disclosed on the officials' statements of economic interests. (Sections 
87200- 87210.) 
• Third, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor 
of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $460 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. 
(Sections 87100 & 87103(e); Regulations 18700 & 18703.4.) 


File No. 1-16-079 
Page No. 4 
Under the Act, any travel is presumed to confer some personal benefit on a public official. 
In addition, travel costs paid for by a third party generally are reportable gifts under the Act. 
Therefore, absent an exception, the value of all travel-related expenses to China will be considered 
a reportable gift to each of the Mayors. (Section 82028; Gault Advice Letter, No. A-07-158.) 
2. Exceptions 
Under some circumstances, even though a payment for transportation, lodging, and 
subsistence may be a reportable gift under the Act, the gift is not subject to the Act's current $460 
gift limit. In this regard, Section 89506(a) provides (in pertinent part) an exception from the gift 
limits for certain travel payments: 
"Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either of 
the following apply: 
"( l ) .... 
"(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a 
foreign government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private 
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code, a nonprofit organization that is exempt from taxation under Section 
50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside the 
United States who substantially satisfies the requirements for tax - exempt status 
under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code." 
A. Legislative or govemmental purpose, or an issue of state, national, or intemational 
public policy. 
According to the facts, the trip's purpose is to improve business cooperation between China 
and the Silicon Valley, to help create jobs and increase economic activity in both China and the 
Silicon Valley, to facilitate investment and international trade, and to promote communications 
between China and the Silicon Valley region. While in China, the mayors will have opportunities to 
participate in meetings with local government officials, potential investors, and CEOs from local 
high-tech companies. Based on these facts, the travel is directly related to a legislative or 
governmental purpose. 
B. The travel is provided by a govemment, a govemmental agency, a foreign govenzment, a 
govemmental authority. 
Additionally, all international air travel expenses to and from China, approximately $2,000 
per person, and the remaining expenses for travel, lodging and meals within China during the 2016 
China Trip, approximately $3,000 per person, will be provided by local Chinese government 


File No. 1-16-079 
Page No. 5 
authorities, including the Zhangjiang Management Committee, which is the regulatory authority of 
Shanghai Pudong New District in Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park, People's Government of Yubei District 
in Chongqing, and Zhengzhou Municipal People's Government. A non-profit organization 
supervised by Zhongguancun Tech Park Management Council and Haidian District Government in 
Beijing, referred to as the Beijing Digital Content Industries Association, will also be covering 
some of the expenses. 
So long as the and Beijing Digital Content Industries Association substantially satisfies the 
requirements for tax exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, all of the 
sources of funding for the travel would fall within the purview of Section 89506(a)(2). 
Consequently, the specified payments are reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits 
pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2). Please note, however, that any payments made for items other than 
travel and related lodging and subsistence, such as payments for entertainment purposes, may be 
considered a reportable gift subject to the $460 gift limit and the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act because those items would not be related to a legislative or governmental purpose. 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification 
As mentioned above, a public official who receives gift(s) of $460 or more may have a 
financial conflict of interest under the Act. (Sections 87100 et seq.) The official must disqualify 
himself or herself from voting or otherwise participating in a governmental decision affecting that 
source, if the payment was received or promised to the official within 12 months preceding the 
decision. Therefore, if the Mayors accept gifts from the sources identified above valued at $460 or 
more, they may be prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting those sources. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Commission Counsel, 
Legal Division

sections.question: Are the payments for a China trip provided by foreign governments and local government authorities to Silicon Valley mayors subject to the gift limits and conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: All of the payments for international air travel and in-country travel, lodging, and meals are reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2), provided the non-profit organization substantially satisfies Section 501(c)(3) tax-exempt requirements. However, any payments for items other than travel and related lodging and subsistence, such as entertainment, may be subject to the $460 gift limit and conflict of interest provisions.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether travel payments provided by Chinese government authorities and non-profit organizations for a Silicon Valley mayors' trade trip to China constitute reportable gifts subject to California's gift limits and conflict of interest rules.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The travel payments from foreign governments and certain organizations qualify for an exception under Section 89506(a)(2) and are not subject to gift limits because they have a governmental purpose and are provided by qualifying government entities or non-profit organizations. Mayors must disqualify themselves from decisions affecting these sources if gifts total $460 or more within 12 months.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Informal advice letter with clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. Document includes detailed facts, analysis with statutory citations, and addresses both the gift limit exception and conflict of interest disqualification implications. No withdrawal or declination of request.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are the payments for a China trip provided by foreign governments and local government authorities to Silicon Valley mayors subject to the gift limits and conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: All of the payments for international air travel and in-country travel, lodging, and meals are reportable gifts that are not subject to gift limits pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2), provided the non-profit organization substantially satisfies Section 501(c)(3) tax-exempt requirements. However, any payments for items other than travel and related lodging and subsistence, such as entertainment, may be subject to the $460 gift limit and conflict of interest provisions.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides informal advice that travel expenses for a Silicon Valley mayors' business development trip to China, funded by Chinese government authorities and organizations, constitute reportable gifts exempt from gift limits under Section 89506(a)(2). The travel has a governmental purpose and is provided by qualifying foreign governments and potentially tax-exempt organizations, though non-travel entertainment expenses may remain subject to gift limits.
